# Reproducible Reports with Quarto {#sec-quarto}

::: {.callout-note}
## Sources

Stat 545 Chapter 4 (Jenny Bryan, UBC); blog posts
`29-setupquarto`, `07-multilanguagequartodemo`,
`17-rapidconversionRtoRmd`.
:::

## Prerequisites

Answer the following questions to see if you can bypass this
chapter. You can find the answers at the end of the chapter in
@sec-quiz-answers-11.

1. What is 'literate programming', and how does a `.qmd` file
   embody it?
2. Given a Quarto document that contains R code and prose, what
   single command renders it to an HTML file, and how does
   Quarto decide which output format to produce?
3. What is the purpose of `execute: freeze: auto` in a Quarto
   document, and what problem does it solve?

## Learning objectives

By the end of this chapter you should be able to:

- Explain the relationship among R scripts, R Markdown,
  Jupyter notebooks, and Quarto.
- Create a `.qmd` file that produces HTML, PDF, and Word
  outputs from the same source.
- Control code chunks with chunk options.
- Add cross-references to figures, tables, sections, and
  equations that resolve in both HTML and PDF output.
- Use `bibliography` and `csl` to cite published work.
- Parameterise a report with YAML `params:` and render
  multiple variants.
- Cache computation with `freeze` to avoid re-running
  expensive code on every render.

## Orientation

An analysis is not finished when the code runs. It is
finished when a collaborator can reproduce the plots, tables,
and paragraphs of your paper from your source files with one
command. Quarto is the current standard tool for doing that
in R.

## Literate programming

TODO: Knuth's original idea; mixing code and prose; contrast
with the 'paste output into Word' workflow.

## The minimum Quarto document

TODO: a three-line `.qmd` that renders to HTML.

## Code chunks and chunk options

TODO: `echo`, `eval`, `include`, `warning`, `message`,
`fig-cap`, `fig-width`, `cache`.

## Cross-references

TODO: `{#fig-...}`, `{#tbl-...}`, `{#eq-...}`, and the
matching `@fig-...` references.

## YAML front matter

TODO: `title`, `author`, `date`, `format`, `bibliography`,
`execute`, `params`.

## Bibliographies and citations

TODO: linking a `.bib`, `@ref`, `[@ref]`, style via `csl`.

## Caching with freeze

TODO: how `_freeze/` works, when to use `freeze: auto` vs
`freeze: true`.

## With AI assistance {.unnumbered}

::: {.callout-tip}
## Prompts to try

1. Paste the `.qmd` source of a report and ask the LLM to
   add appropriate `fig-cap` and cross-references to each
   figure. Render both versions and diff for unexpected
   changes.
2. Ask the LLM to set up a parameterised report that loops
   over three datasets and generates one HTML file per
   dataset. Check whether it uses Quarto's `params:`
   feature.
3. Give the LLM a Word document of a draft paper and ask
   it to convert the prose into a `.qmd` with placeholders
   for the R code that generated each figure. Verify every
   figure caption survives.
:::

## Exercises

1. Convert a short `.R` script into a `.qmd` that renders
   to both HTML and PDF, with one figure caption and one
   cross-reference.
2. Add `bibliography: references.bib` and cite at least
   one paper. Verify the reference appears in both HTML
   and PDF outputs.
3. Parameterise the document to take a `data_file`
   parameter from YAML; render three versions from three
   different CSV files using
   `quarto render --to html -P data_file=foo.csv`.

## Further reading

- Quarto documentation at `quarto.org` --- canonical reference.
- Xie, Dervieux, and Riederer (2020), *R Markdown Cookbook* --- recipes that largely translate to Quarto.

## Quiz answers {#sec-quiz-answers-11}

1. Literate programming (coined by Knuth) interleaves code
   with the prose that explains it, so the same source
   file is the authoritative record of both the
   computation and its interpretation. A Quarto `.qmd`
   file embodies this by letting you write narrative
   Markdown and executable R (or Python, Julia) code
   chunks in one document.
2. `quarto render file.qmd` produces the default output
   format declared in the document's YAML `format:` key
   (HTML if no `format:` is present). To force a format,
   use `quarto render file.qmd --to html` (or `--to pdf`,
   `--to docx`).
3. `execute: freeze: auto` caches the executed output of
   each code chunk into `_freeze/` on first render.
   Subsequent renders reuse the cached output if the chunk
   source is unchanged, avoiding re-running expensive code
   during every render and making CI deployments fast.
